Hi, first of all, thanks for the work on the project, it's very usefull to have an HMM framework in python. 

Regarding the disclaimer on the landing page: "For supervised learning learning of HMMs and similar models see seqlearn." 

What do you think about using the framework for a classification problem with labeled data?. The way I think it could work is by building one HMM per class (trained with the specific class data) and then, when data has to be predicted, compare the scores of each model and select the best. 

For example, in a two class classification problem, something like this: (didn't test it)

```python

from hmmlearn import hmm
import numpy as np

class CustomHMM:
  def __init__(self):
      def build_hmm():
          model = hmm.GMMHMM(n_components=3, n_mix=3, covariance_type="diag", init_params="t")
          model.transmat_ = np.array([[0.5, 0.5, 0.0],
                                      [0.0, 0.5, 0.5],
                                      [0.0, 0.0, 1.0]])
          return model

      self.hmm_0 = build_hmm()
      self.hmm_1 = build_hmm()

  def fit(self, X_train, y_train):
      # X_train shape(n_instances, n_samples)
      labels = set(y_train)
      if len(labels) != 2:
          raise Exception("y_train doesn't contain 2 classes")
      X_0 = X_train[y_train == 0, :]
      X_1 = X_train[y_train == 1, :]

      self.hmm_0.fit(X_0)
      self.hmm_1.fit(X_1)

  def predict(self, X_test):
      res = []
      for x in X_test:
           res.append(0 if self.hmm_0.score(x) > self.hmm_1.score(x) else 1)
      return np.array(res)
```
Then, you can use it:

```python
clf = CustomHMM()
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
clf.predict(X_test)

issue_title: HTTPS requests with proxy broken, because HTTP status line include full URL
issue_body:
## Long story short

When I used aiohttp with proxy to fetch https resource, I always got only the front page of the requested domain, because aiohttp wrongly included domain in the eventual get request.

## Expected behaviour

```
CONNECT example.com:443 HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
User-Agent: Python/3.6 aiohttp/3.0.1
```

```
GET /some/path HTTP/1.1
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Host: example.com
User-Agent: Python/3.6 aiohttp/3.0.1
```

## Actual behaviour

```
CONNECT example.com:443 HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
User-Agent: Python/3.6 aiohttp/3.0.1
```

```
GET https://example.com/some/path HTTP/1.1
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Host: example.com
User-Agent: Python/3.6 aiohttp/3.0.1
```

I located the issue to this commit:

https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/commit/3f283c624e39da7fd45457646a82854e83bd7ebe

And patched it locally with:

```
diff --git a/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py b/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py
index 33bd25ca..31c0ce93 100644
--- a/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py
+++ b/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py
@@ -472,7 +472,7 @@ class ClientRequest:
         # - most common is origin form URI
         if self.method == hdrs.METH_CONNECT:
             path = '{}:{}'.format(self.url.raw_host, self.url.port)
-        elif self.proxy and not self.ssl:
+        elif self.proxy and not self.ssl and not self.url.scheme == 'https':
             path = str(self.url)
         else:
             path = self.url.raw_path
```

Not sure what would be the optimal test to separate proxied http requests from proxied https requests.

issue_title: URL Resolver methods should not reference private attributes of parameters passed in
issue_body:
in https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py there are a variety of places where `_method` is pulled from the request instead of `.method`.  This in particular is causing me an issue when I try to pass a `RawRequestMessage` to my custom resolver (resolving each request of a google batch API call) as it only has `method` and not `_method`

see for example: 
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py#L510
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py#L702
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py#L762

this is both bad design (referencing private attribute) and unneeded from what I see, they should just use `request.method`.

issue_title: Dynamic models: Possible memory issues because of _subclasses

In https://github.com/schematics/schematics/blob/v2.0.1/schematics/models.py#L118, we find this code

```python
        # Register class on ancestor models

issue_title: cwltool use of singularity fails with bioconda containers with default output directory (/var/spool/cwl)
issue_body:
## Expected Behavior
When cwltool uses a container, it maps a directory on the host filesystem to a working directory path within the container. By default this path is `/var/spool/cwl`. This is passed to the singularity command line where it is used in a bind-mount and for the `--pwd` parameter. For the CWL code below it yields:

```
singularity \
    --quiet \
    exec \
    --contain \
    --pid \
    --ipc \
    --bind \
    /tmp/tmpmMeF3o:/var/spool/cwl:rw \
    --bind \
    /tmp/tmp6fUHbo:/tmp:rw \
    --pwd \
    /var/spool/cwl \
    /some/path/quay.io-biocontainers-blast-2.7.1--boost1.64_1.img \
    blastp \
    . > /tmp/tmpmMeF3o/listing.txt
```

This is all correct from the `cwltool` side. It leads to a failure with bioconda containers, as described below.
## Actual Behavior
Bioconda containers are built on a `busybox` base container where `/var/spool` is a symbolic link to `/var/tmp` which itself is a symbolic link to `/tmp`.

Singularity does a bind-mount between the host system's `/tmp` and `/tmp` within the container. Because of the symbolic links described above, `/var/spool/cwl` is effectively `/tmp` so the second bind-mount fails (silently). The result is that inside the container `/var/spool/cwl` does not exist, resulting in the error shown in the traceback. I have reported the Singularity issue: https://github.com/singularityware/singularity/issues/1833

A workaround is to use a `dockerRequirement` under `requirements` and specify a `dockerOutputDirectory` that does not clash with an existing bind mount. E.g.

```
requirements:
   - class: DockerRequirement
     dockerPull: quay.io/biocontainers/blast:2.7.1--boost1.64_1
     dockerOutputDirectory: /var/cwl
```

## Workflow Code
```
cwlVersion: v1.0
class: CommandLineTool

hints:
  - class: DockerRequirement
    dockerPull: quay.io/biocontainers/blast:2.7.1--boost1.64_1

inputs:
  filename:
    type: string
    default: "."
    inputBinding:
      position: 1

outputs:
  output_file:
    type: File
    outputBinding:
      glob: listing.txt

stdout: listing.txt

baseCommand: ls
```
## Full Traceback
```
$ cwltool --singularity example_failure.cwl 
/tools/software/toil_venv/bin/cwltool 1.0.20180809224403
Resolved 'example_failure.cwl' to 'file:///usr/people/pvh/mapula/scripts/example_failure.cwl'
Using local copy of Singularity image found in /usr/people/pvh/mapula/scripts
[job example_failure.cwl] /tmp/tmpmMeF3o$ singularity \
    --quiet \
    exec \
    --contain \
    --pid \
    --ipc \
    --bind \
    /tmp/tmpmMeF3o:/var/spool/cwl:rw \
    --bind \
    /tmp/tmp6fUHbo:/tmp:rw \
    --pwd \
    /var/spool/cwl \
    /some/path/quay.io-biocontainers-blast-2.7.1--boost1.64_1.img \
    blastp \
    . > /tmp/tmpmMeF3o/listing.txt
ERROR  : Could not change directory to: /var/spool/cwl
ABORT  : Retval = 255
[job example_failure.cwl] completed permanentFail
{
    "output_file": {
        "checksum": "sha1$da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709",

issue_title: Single Quotes in config are not escaped
issue_body:
Single quotes in the config dict are not escaped.

Code
`'config': json_encode(self.config)`

HTML
`data-config='{{config|safe}}'`

json_encode does not escape single quotes, therefore config dicts with single quotes invalidates the data-config attribute. This leads to default configuration with all the plugins enabled.

Suggested code
`'config': json_encode(self.config).replace('\'', ''')`

Add description to fields of marshmallow generated schema
issue_body:
Is it possible to add additional fields to OpenAPI schemas generated via marshmallow `Schema`. One example would be descriptions for fields. 

```
class ASchema(Schema):
    a = fields.Int()
```
```
    ASchema:
      properties:
        a:
          type: integer
          description: 'description for a field'
      type: object
      description: 'description for a schema'
```
Currently docstrings on schemas are ignored, correct?

When supplying a float value to a schema whose field is registered as `Integer`, Marshmallow will implicitly cast the given float to an integer, without raising errors, even when attempting to force strictness.

e.g.: a given value of 0.15 will be silently cast to 0, of type `int`.

This behaviour comes from the definition of the constructor for the Integer field. Line 707 in `fields.py`:

```
# override Number
    def __init__(self, strict=False, **kwargs):
        self.strict = strict
        super(Integer, self).__init__(**kwargs)
```

`strict=False` is the culprit. 

Expected output would be: `ValidationError: {'your_value': [u'Not a valid integer.']`}

Thoughts?

issue_title: Validating fields across schemas with Schema.validator
missing and default values should be specified in deserialized form
issue_body:
Marshmallow expects `missing` and `default` values to be specified in a pre-serialized form, which is inconvenient and unintuitive.

Here is a schema which I would expect to work:

```python
import datetime
import uuid

class TestSchema(Schema):
    id = fields.UUID(
        missing=uuid.uuid1)
    ts = fields.DateTime(
        format='rfc',
        missing=datetime.datetime.now)
```

However, it does not:

```python
schema = TestSchema()
pprint.pprint(tuple(schema.load({})))
({}, {'id': [u'Not a valid UUID.'], 'ts': [u'Not a valid datetime.']})
```

Instead, I have to specify `defaults` and `missing` values as primitive types, which undermines the purpose of a library like marshmallow that already knows how to do this.  For data types like datetimes, which have many possible serialized formats, I have to keep the formatting of my `default` and `missing` values in sync with my field properties, which is fragile and convoluted:

```python
from email.Utils import formatdate

class TestSchema(Schema):
    id = fields.UUID(
        missing=lambda: str(uuid.uuid1()))
    ts = fields.DateTime(
        format='rfc',
        missing=formatdate)
```

In the case of `default` values, marshmallow does not attempt to validate them, so no error is raised, but the resulting document is not serializable.

issue_title: Match behaviour of closing LoomConnection with regular file API behaviour
issue_body:
Just a quick check if these API updates are approved before I push them to GitHub:

Normal file connections have a read-only `closed` Bool variable that the user can read to check if the file is closed. Added this to `LoomConnection` too. Basically, `self._closed` is set to false by `__init__`, and to true by `close()`, and accessible through a `closed` getter:

```python
	@property
	def closed(self) -> Bool:
		return self._closed
```

Currently, calling `ds.close()` on an already closed connection raises an error because it calls `self._file.close()` after setting `self._file` to none.

I'm somewhat on the fence on this one, since throwing an error could arguably be considered the right behaviour (telling the programmer to not call `close()` superfluously). 

OTOH, trying to close an already closed connection is harmless, and very annoying if it interrupts a long-running script. Also, calling `f.close()` twice on a regular file does nothing, so that's apparently the Pythonic way that users will expect.

The lack of feedback is a bit stupid IMO, so as a compromise I added a check + a warning for now:

```python
	def close(self) -> None:
		"""
		Close the connection. After this, the connection object becomes invalid.
		"""
		if self._file is None:
			# Warn user that they're being paranoid and should clean up their code
			logging.warn("Connection to %s is already closed", self.filename)
		else:
			self._file.close()
			self._file = None
		self.row_attrs = {}
		self.col_attrs = {}
		self.shape = [0, 0]
		self.closed = true
```

Finally, I wrapped `__init__` in a `try/except` block to ensure we properly close the h5py connection in case it fails on bad input (semi-related to issue #10):

```python
		try:
			# old initialisation code
		except e:
			logging.warn("construction LoomConnection to %s failed, closing file connection", filename)
			self.close()
			raise e
```

> The input tensor in forward(input) is expected to be a 5D tensor (batch x time x nInputPlane x height x width). output of forward(input) is also expected to be a 5D tensor (batch x time x outputPlane x height x width).

We should add such specs for:

    parser.add_option("-m", "--modelPath", dest="modelPath", default="")

Let's try to agree on the variable names and capitalization rules for all terms in the library. PEP8 is a good place to start, but it may be reasonable to break those rules for some terms. 

I don't expect that we can come to agreement on everything in this issue, but let's at least write down the terms and rules for which we do agree and change the code to reflect whatever consensus emerges. 

A wiki would probably be a good place to put the results. 

In no particular order, here are some problems that I'm aware of:

* Acronyms such as DNI, GHI, DHI, AOI. 

* Beam irradiance and similar could be ``Eb``, ``beam``, ``beam_irrad``, ``irrad_beam``. I know that at least two those are currently in use.

* ``diffuse``, ``sky_diffuse``, ``diffuse_ground``, ``In_Plane_SkyDiffuse``, ``aoi_projection``, etc.

* ``airmass``, ``AM``, or ``am``? I think we may be using all three. How do we use a modifier like relative or absolute?

* ``lat``, ``latitude``

* ``tz``, ``TZ``

* ``surf_az``, ``surfazi``, ``surfaceazimuth``

* Variables that are closely tied to a particular equation (see many of the ``pvsystem`` functions).

* ``temp``, ``T``, ``Tcell``. 

* ``Vmp``, ``V_mp``, ``vmp``, ``v_mp``.

Fixing this without breaking things requires good tests.

jsonapi-requests is not JSON API compatible. Because if server returns this relationship obejct:

> "relationships":{"vendor":{"links":{"self":"http://example.org/api/rest/admin/gateway-groups/14/relationships/vendor","related":"http://example.org/api/rest/admin/gateway-groups/14/vendor"}}}
Empty relationship object in ApiResponse

But in http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-resource-object-relationships

> 
> A “relationship object” MUST contain at least one of the following:
> 
>     links: a links object containing at least one of the following:
>         self: a link for the relationship itself (a “relationship link”). This link allows the client to directly manipulate the relationship. For example, removing an author through an article’s relationship URL would disconnect the person from the article without deleting the people resource itself. When fetched successfully, this link returns the linkage for the related resources as its primary data. (See Fetching Relationships.)
>         related: a related resource link
>     data: resource linkage
>     meta: a meta object that contains non-standard meta-information about the relationship
Project must support this.

issue_title: distribute-forall tactic distributes even existential quantifiers
issue_body:
The tactic `distribute-forall` distributes any quantifier (even existential) over conjunction and therefore produces incorrect results. For example given

```
(assert 
    (exists ((a (_ BitVec 32)))
          (and (= a #x00000000) (not (= a #x00000000)))
     )
)
```

the result of `(apply distribute-forall)` is

```
(goals
(goal
  (exists ((a (_ BitVec 32))) (= a #x00000000))
  (exists ((a (_ BitVec 32))) (not (= a #x00000000)))
  :precision precise :depth 1)
)
```

This transformation is not correct and causes wrong results. For example, asserting the same formula, the result of 

```
(check-sat-using (then distribute-forall smt))
(check-sat-using smt))
```

is

```
sat
unsat
```

I have tested the stable version (4.4.0) and the unstable version `ed806b6` and both have the same problem. 

I believe the way to fix this issue is to add condition `old_q->is_forall() ` to lines 34 and 56 in the file `z3/src/tactic/core/distribute_forall_tactic.cpp`.

issue_title: Z3 timeouts on an example 4.5.0 could decide
issue_body:
Hello,

I think Z3 (master version, nightly binaries) is less efficient than earlier versions (4.4.2 and 4.5.0). On my [example.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1505833/example.txt), Z3 takes a lot of time. With previous version, it answered in less than 1 second.

Is it expected ?

I've been working a bit on Bayesian Neural Networks in PyMC3 (here is the blog post: http://twiecki.github.io/blog/2016/06/01/bayesian-deep-learning/). I wonder if it would be possible to use Lasagne for construction of the layers. The only requirement would be to place priors (theano expressions) on the weight parameters and feed the output into a likelihood function. Not sure if and how easy it would be to adapt Lasagne in that way.

```

This is occurring on OSX which has case insensitive paths. FTPFS meta has `"case_insensitive" : False`.

I don't know of an easy way to detect the case sensitivity of an ftp server. AFAICT its not exposed.

issue_title: [Bug] Video keep playing if terminal crashes or is closed without first quitting mps-youtube
issue_body: I'm not sure if anything can be done about this but I accidently force closed the wrong terminal window and was left with an hour long song playing in the background without any way of stopping it.

#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```
from glob import glob
from satpy.scene import Scene
from satpy.utils import debug_on
debug_on()

FILES = glob("/home/a000680/data/JPSS-1/*h5")

scn = Scene(filenames=FILES, reader='viirs_sdr')
composite = 'true_color_lowres'
scn.load([composite])
```

#### Problem description

Running the above code generates a nice looking True Color image. However, in the log I see the below output! So, Pyspectral cannot find the correct RSR file, since the platform-name is wrong ('J01').

#### Expected Output

#### Actual Result, Traceback if applicable
```
[INFO: 2017-12-22 09:57:57 : satpy.composites] Removing Rayleigh scattering and aerosol absorption

[ERROR: 2017-12-22 09:57:58 : pyspectral.rayleigh] No spectral responses for this platform and sensor: J01 viirs
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/a000680/usr/src/pyspectral/pyspectral/rayleigh.py", line 134, in get_effective_wavelength
    rsr = RelativeSpectralResponse(self.platform_name, self.sensor)
  File "/home/a000680/usr/src/pyspectral/pyspectral/rsr_reader.py", line 91, in __init__
    raise IOError(errmsg)
IOError: pyspectral RSR file does not exist! Filename = /home/a000680/data/pyspectral/rsr_viirs_J01.h5
Files matching instrument and satellite platform: []

#### Versions of Python, package at hand and relevant dependencies

Python 2.7.5, Pyspectral v0.5.1

We need a test which actually uses the .exe parameter in the Executable program to submit a job.

I suspect this may be broken in some cases in 6.1.19 but we don't have a test which properly confirms this as far as I'm aware and we really should. The behaviour of this class with the default settings and the 'normal' use-case is extremely different.

If it shows up in 6.1.19 testing I suggest we fix it but if there isn't then I've probably found a corner case.

Also this should be run on multiple backends at some point as the RT/App doesn't ship the files as expected.

issue_body:
* Before opening a new issue, we wanted to provide you with some useful suggestions (Click "Preview" above for a better view):

    * Consider checking out SDK [examples](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/master/examples#sample-code-for-intel-realsense-cameras).

* All users are welcomed to report bugs, ask questions, suggest or request enhancements and generally feel free to open new issue, even if they haven't followed any of the suggestions above :)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | { D400 } | 
| Firmware Version                   | (Open RealSense Viewer --> Click info) | 
| Operating System & Version |   Ubuntu 16.04  | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    |  4.13.0                                     | 
| Platform                                 | PC |
| SDK Version                            |  { legacy / 2.<10>.<4> }                          | 


### Issue Description
<Describe your issue / question / feature request / etc..>
I want to design a UI interface with Qt to change the exposure and gain using spinBox like the simple RealSenseViewer. But when I want to transmit the pictures of D435 to Qt interface and deliver the data of spinBox to D435, I ran into trouble. Can someone give me a advice?
Thanks a lot.
I think it's time. :smile:

On the `pip install` compilation:

```
x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc -pthread -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O2 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -Qunused-arguments\n\036  -Wno-unused-function -Wdate-time -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fPIC -I/usr/include/python3.5m -I/home/cacilhas/tmp/test/__/include/python3.5m -c httptools/parser/parser.c -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.5/httptools/parser/parser.o -O2
  x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc: error: unrecognized command line option ‘-Qunused-arguments\n\036  -Wno-unused-function’
  error: command 'x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc' failed with exit status 1
```

The `-Qunused-arguments\n` parameter has a LF and a bizarre 001E characters at the end as part of the parameter, making GCC to fail.
In your uvloop docs you talk about running aiohttp with this parser instead of the built in one. Do you have any documentation on how that would work?

issue_title: v1.0: Lazily load model class in Field and relay.ConnectionField as in v0.x
issue_body:
Currently rewriting our GraphQL API to work with graphene 1.0 but I'm coming across a big problem:

In order to create the models, I've written a construction class that will convert the actual database models to models used by graphene. There arises a problem however when creating relations on the models using the Field and relay.ConnectionField classes. In graphene 0.x I would be able to provide the name string as argument to Field and relay.ConnectionField, but apparently this is not possible anymore in 1.0 (I'm wondering why you took this out as well). 

It's impossible to import the model classes in the construction class due to circular dependencies. I'm hoping there is another solution to this problem, other than completely rewriting everything to something that is not logically structured at all anymore. Is this something that is going to change in the future or is there a fix available?

JSON doesn't have a date format so dates in graphene are just string fields. This means any dates that are output without being formatted first (e.g. if you use a DjangoNode with only_fields where one of the fields is a date field) are just output as `unicode(date_object)` (which looks like `2016-03-29 17:42:01.683650+00:00`). If you take that date in JavaScript and do `new Date(myDateStr)` browsers will probably work it out but it's not an officially supported date format (as we found out when we tried to parse it in React Native - i.e. Javascript Core on iOS - which doesn't handle it).

I'm happy to create a pull request but I'm not sure what the best solution would be. Here are some options:
 * Make all dates output as `2016-10-01T14:12:00+01:00` which is a format JavaScript understands (which makes sense since we're outputting JSON)
     * if we go down this route I'm not sure where the best place to hook it in would be - maybe another special case in coerce_string in graphql-core like there is for booleans?
     * another option would be to just do it for Django fields - make a DateTimeString type with a separate serialize function and modify convert_django_field for the DateField
 * Do one of the above but make it configurable somehow
 * If those are't options (e.g. for backwards compatibility) then it would be nice to be able to hook in and do it ourselves without having to think about a fix every time we encounter a date field

Happy to do the work. If you'd like that, let me know which option you'd prefer and I'll open a PR.

Since we use slotted classes for ASTs, we need a way to convert an AST `Node` to a Dict. 

I.e. `Name(value='test')` Should get converted to `{"kind": "Name", "value": "test"}`. 

It should convert recursively.

issue_title: Tensorboard does not recognize absolute GCP storage path in projector_config
issue_body:
**System specifications**

- TensorBoard version: 0.1.8
 - TensorFlow version: 1.3.0
 - OS Platform and version: Ubuntu 16.04 subsystem on Windows 10 [wsl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Subsystem_for_Linux)
 - Python version: 2.7

**Problem specification**

I am using a projector_config.pbtxt file to link my embedding tensors to their metadata. The config file is structured like this:

```
embeddings {
  tensor_name: "scope/first_tensor_name:0"
  metadata_path: "gs://bucket/not-logdir/first_dict"
}
embeddings {
  tensor_name: "scope/second_tensor_name:0"
  metadata_path: "gs://bucket/not-logdir/second_dict"
}
...
```

When I load the projector in tensorboard, I get the following error message:

> **Error fetching metadata**
> "gs://bucket/logdir/gs://bucket/not-logdir/first_dict" not found, or is not a file

Tensorboard uses my absolute path as a relative path. 

**Source code**

The metadata path is fetched [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/f6bb9b09371c2b1e2107ac5c26b205528eb0ff08/tensorboard/plugins/projector/projector_plugin.py#L458) and constructed to an absolute path [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/f6bb9b09371c2b1e2107ac5c26b205528eb0ff08/tensorboard/plugins/projector/projector_plugin.py#L203):

```
def _rel_to_abs_asset_path(fpath, config_fpath):
  fpath = os.path.expanduser(fpath)
  if not os.path.isabs(fpath):
    return os.path.join(os.path.dirname(config_fpath), fpath)
  return fpath
```

This snippet shows that the path should only be made absolute if it is not an absolute path already.
The `os.path.isabs()` function however fails to detect if a GCP storage path is absolute.

Is there another way to specify a metadata path outside of the logdir on GCP storage or should this be fixed?

The relevant code seems to be unchanged between my version 0.1.8 and the current version 1.6.0.

issue_title: String variable expansion incorrectly adds double quotes in SQL
issue_body: When using name expansion in a SQL query, if the expanded variable is a string, it's replaced with `"value"`, keeping the double quotes, which is a syntax error in SQL.

## Problem Definition

The `test_transfer_succesful_after_secret_learned` is not yet implemented.

## Solution

Implement it


## Tasklist

- [ ] Implement `test_transfer_succesful_after_secret_learned()` in test_target

## Problem Definition

After trying several options to monkey-patch gevent, it became clearer gevent is not intended to work like we're currently using it, spawning and forgetting greenlets and missing possible unhandled exceptions. Gevent doesn't provide a central point for handling unhandled exceptions, as `gevent.get_hub().handle_error` is called on every exception, despite it being handled after by `try/except` around `Greenlet.get` or by a `link_exception` callback.

The _right_ way to do it is to, when **needed** to spawn a greenlet, be strict about keeping track of its lifecycle, and `.get`ing its result when possible, to be sure any exception raised inside will be either handled or propagated to parent, and do that up to the **long-lived** parent greenlet (greenlets that are expected to keep running during the entire lifecycle of the app, usually with `while True` loops), and from there to the `NodeRunner`, which will then crash the app with the right traceback and make sure possible cleanup tasks are called.

Obs: `link_exception` is not suited for exception handling alone, as it can't change the greenlet status, and the callback won't be executed in the parent's context (as `Greenlet.get`), which will make any exception on itself to be lost. It may be useful for notifying, logging or other tasks like that.

This will make sure we don't lose exceptions anymore, won't require any blackmagic/behavior-changing gevent monkey-patch, and will have sane and transparent exception handling all through the codebase, where `try/except` blocks will work as expected.

## Proposal:

- [ ] Track long-lived greenlets in [NodeRunner](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/077ba5445dae152333c63f062287d42ff8d396cf/raiden/ui/cli.py#L999) and make sure to handle and re-raise any exception on them
  - [ ] [AlarmTask](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/077ba5445dae152333c63f062287d42ff8d396cf/raiden/raiden_service.py#L165)
  - [ ] [Transport thread](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/077ba5445dae152333c63f062287d42ff8d396cf/raiden/network/transport/matrix.py#L202)
  - [ ] [check_version](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/077ba5445dae152333c63f062287d42ff8d396cf/raiden/ui/cli.py#L986)
  - [ ] [check_gas_reserve](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/077ba5445dae152333c63f062287d42ff8d396cf/raiden/ui/cli.py#L988)
- [ ] Make sure unhandled exceptions in [APIServer](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/077ba5445dae152333c63f062287d42ff8d396cf/raiden/ui/cli.py#L963) and `RestAPI` are bubbled up to crash the API server and the `NodeRunner` itself.
- [ ] Ensure **every** spawned greenlet throughout the application have a clear lifecycle, being eventually `get`ed (as close to the spawning point, as better), and possible raised exceptions either handled or bubbled up to the parent node (`Greenlet.get` will re-raise the exception)
Currently the keyid for aes128gcm is being passed as base64 encoded instead of raw values. We shouldn't pass a keyid at all, really.

Addressing this will make our codebase more maintainable and improve [Better Code Hub](https://bettercodehub.com)'s **Couple Architecture Components Loosely** guideline rating! 👍 

Here's the gist of this guideline:
- **Definition** 📖 
Minimize the amount of interface code; that is, code in modules that are both called from and call modules of other components ("throughput"), and code in modules that are called from modules of other components ("incoming").
- **Why**❓ 
Having loose coupling between top-level components makes it easier to maintain them in isolation.
- **How** 🔧 
You can hide a component's implementation details through various means, e.g. using the "abstract factory" design pattern.

You can find more info about this guideline in [Building Maintainable Software](http://shop.oreilly.com/product/0636920049159.do). 📖

We now have function to_docker, which translates local path names to remote path names using `path_map` runtime option or `CONFIG['hosts'][host]['path_map']`. issue_title: Set attribute using LazyAttribute only if another attribute exists
issue_body:
Hello,
I have this kind of Django model:

```
from djmoney.models.fields import MoneyField
from moneyed import Money

class Item(models.Model):
    total_due = MoneyField(max_digits=17,
                           decimal_places=7,
                           default_currency=DEFAULT_CURRENCY,
                           currency_choices=CURRENCIES_CHOICES,
                           null=True, blank=True)

    c_total_due = = MoneyField(max_digits=17,
                           decimal_places=7,
                           default_currency=DEFAULT_CURRENCY,
                           currency_choices=CURRENCIES_CHOICES,
                           null=True, blank=True)
```

The field `c_total_due` is computed from `total_due` so its value is always related to it.

Given that, if the `total_due` is set then I would like my factory to generate the the `c_total_due` to be exactly the same as `total_due` if not explicitly set:

```
class ItemFactory(factory.DjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = Item

    c_total_due = factory.LazyAttribute(lambda o: o.total_due)
```

The problem is that if I don't explicitly create a factory object by passing a `total_due` this will fail.
```
AttributeError: The parameter total_charged is unknown.
```

Is there a way to set a value of an attribute based on another one using the `LazyAttribute` lambda only if the first attribute is set in the first place?

I hope I was clear : ]

issue_title: SFTP False error raised during open
issue_body:
Using git version (master) of the `ssh2-python`, the sftp open code doesn't handle the `EAGAIN` and raise it as an error.

I made a small patch which fix the issue on my local setup, which was raising all the time an error when trying to use sftp, because of the `EAGAIN`, but this is probably but the most efficient way you want to commit (this is why I didn't made any Pull Request).

The patch: https://github.com/maxux/ssh2-python/commit/7dc38339f70bf7af848ee5e6f33345feb20043dd

Based on the C exemple here: http://yoyakuweb.com/lib/libssh2-1.4.3/libssh2-1.4.3/example/sftp_nonblock.c
Some `waitsocket` workaround should be done and not a loop like me.

Anyway, the `EAGAIN` should definitively be handled during sftp open call.

I'm not sure anybody is looking at the google group anymore, so I'll try to move this here to github.

I have some existing code using paramiko and was doing some tests to replace it. Unfortunately the round trip time of individual commands seems to be quite a bit slower using pssh.
My test code can be found here: https://gist.github.com/mbarkhau/e4f3dde91bbb137b29973c362e2c128e

These are the results

```
                      min [ms]    max [ms]    avg [ms]
paramiko                  5.60       10.73        7.46
pssh run_command         24.18       69.31       56.47
pssh execute             54.36       59.10       56.12
     open                40.79       43.24       42.19
     cmd                 13.37       15.86       13.92
```

It looks like the command execution itself is comparable to paramiko, but I haven't found a way to use the API without doing open_session for every single command.

Is there a different way to use the libraries (maybe ssh2-python directly) which would have comparable/better RTT vs paramiko? Maybe this issue can be resolved with improvements to documentation.

issue_title: [V3 Docs] Remove existing warnings and raise our standards
issue_body:
Warnings during the sphinx build process are a good way for us to know when something is wrong with the docs.

However, the build already raises  a couple warnings. We should really remove these warnings, before we start doing test builds for the docs with tox, as discussed in #1636. This is what I mean by "raising our standards": causing the build to fail on warnings. This should to help prevent most issues in our docs.

# Bug in sphinx 1.6.5
There is a bug in sphinx 1.6.5 which occurs with forward references, which are currently being used for static typing. This bug is fixed in sphinx 1.7. We won't resolve all sphinx build warnings until we either upgrade, or remove the culprit forward reference(s). My preference is the former.

Currently, sphinx is locked to version 1.6.5. The reason for this is that discord.py *also* locks sphinx to 1.6.5, and *their* reason for that is because they use `|coro|` syntax in their docstrings which is no longer supported in sphinx 1.7+.

However, this issue will only come about if we try to document something from the discord.py library, which we should *never* do. An example of how this might occur is when we subclass an object from discord.py and overwrite its methods, but don't add docstrings for some of the methods.

A current example of this is `redbot.core.bot.RedBase.get_context()`. We don't document this method because it's exactly the same as `discord.BotBase.get_context()` except it injects our custom Context class. I think the solution to this, however, is to simply skip this method when documenting `BotBase`. This allows us to upgrade to sphinx 1.7.

issue_title: Should ContextVar changes inside a nursery block be preserved after the nursery ends?
issue_body:
@oremanj raised [an excellent question on gitter](https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=5ad163b66d7e07082bf1dca6) ([see also](https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=5ad17b9327c509a7740e6314)): we generally try to make the code that runs inside an `async with` nursery block "act like" its a task inside the nursery. One place where this falls down is that the nursery block shares a `ContextVars` context with the outer process, so for example:

```python
async def main():
    cvar = contextvars.ContextVar()
    cvar.set("outer")
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        assert cvar.get() == "outer"
        cvar.set("inner")
    assert cvar.get() == "inner"
```

OTOH if we move the nursery block into its own task, then we get different behavior:

```python
async def main():
    cvar = contextvars.ContextVar()
    cvar.set("outer")
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        async def inner():
            assert cvar.get() == "outer"
            cvar.set("inner")
        nursery.start_soon(inner)
    assert cvar.get() == "outer"     # XXXXXX notice this line is different!
```

Is this a bug? Should the code inside the nursery block get its own shallow-copied `Context`, like child tasks do? I am genuinely not sure!

Technically it's certainly possible, though as @oremanj points out there is a a bit of trickiness required: in order to change contexts, you have to round-trip through the run loop (so that it can do `Context.run` with the new context). Currently nursery creation (the `__aenter__` method) is async-colored but is documented not to checkpoint; we could preserve this (if we care) by having a special change-`Context` trap that yields to the run loop but doesn't check for cancellation and immediately resumes the same task without going through the scheduler.

Really, though, it comes down to what users find least confusing, and I don't have a strong intuition here at all.

There are other places where the pseudo-task inside the nursery block is "special". For example, introspection APIs like `task.parent_nursery` reveal the messy reality. I've wondered if we should change this somehow, for example by actually creating a child `Task` object for the code inside the block (and unscheduling the parent `Task` until the child finishes). This would mean the two `Task` objects share the same coroutine object, which is kinda weird, but technically possible. However, even then, there are still cracks in the facade: Python's stack introspection still works the same, so if a debugger tried to get a traceback on those two tasks, then instead of seeing the parent's stack on the parent task and the child's stack on the child task, it would see two copies of the combined parent+child stack, which is pretty confusing. (Maybe we could hack this by providing our own traceback extraction functionality and keeping track of which frame created the nursery...?)

And, of course, Python's scoping rules still apply, so code inside the `async with` block can see and mutate local variables defined outside it. (Which is very similar to this issue, just with s/ContextVar/local var/!) So we're never going to make the nursery block *exactly* like a child task... again, I'm not sure :-).

issue_title: Should we be sharing contextvars.Context between fixtures?
issue_body:
In #50, we started using a [gross hack](https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/blob/dd27d702d7b95e7dc15874a318500aa0c3c5d65d/pytest_trio/plugin.py#L189-L198) to make it so within each test invocation, all fixtures + the test itself all shared a single `contextvars.Context`. This simulates them all running in a single task (except that some can happen concurrently, see #57).

Is this the optimal behavior? I guess the options are:

* Use a single `Context` for all fixtures
* Use separate `Context`s for different fixtures, but when a fixture starts up copy in the values from the previous fixture. (Doing this with concurrent setup/teardown is [pretty complicated](https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/pull/50#issuecomment-414648141); doing it with sequential setup/teardown would be easy.)

The difference is in code like:

```python
mycv = contextvars.ContextVar("mycv")
@trio_fixture
def fix():
    mycv.set("a")
    yield
    assert mycv.get() == "a"

@pytest.mark.trio
async def test(fix):
     mycv.set("b")
```

Right now, the `assert` fails, because the fixture can "see" the `set` call in `test`, because they share a `Context`. If we used separate contexts with copy-on-startup, then `fix` would not be able to see changes made by downstream fixtures. You can think of it as arranging for `ContextVar` changes to be automatically rolled back during the teardown process. This is conceptually attractive, but I don't know if it matters, or is even beneficial, in practice.

Also, even if we keep the current semantics, we should find a way to stop doing the gross hack that we're doing right now. This might mean enhancing trio, e.g. by adding a `context=` argument to `nursery.start_soon`.

For the use cases we have a solution like https://github.com/ianare/exif-py or https://github.com/hMatoba/Piexif would be preferrable so users (and tools depending on OCR-D/core as a lib) do not have to install `libimage-exiftool-perl`.
